In recent years, the field of AI-driven autonomous software engi-
2
: neering has been undergoing a transformative shift [4, 7, 18]. Agents
v powered by large language models have demonstrated the ability
i
to perform a wide range of complex software engineering tasks,
X
r  including code generation [8, 10] and program repair [46, 49]. Re-
a searchers have proposed various agentic methods [27, 32, 40, 44, 48]
to address issues in software repositories, leveraging capabilities
such as fault localization, unit test execution, and code file modifica-
tion to navigate to relevant locations within codebases and generate
patches. Issue localization serves as the foundation for performing
correct edits. However, because of the limited alignment capabilities
of large language models between natural language (NL) and pro-
gramming languages (PL) [18], accurately navigating to suspicious
locations based on issue reports remains a significant challenge. For
example, in the SWE-bench dataset [11], users often describe their
requirements using underspecified natural language [25], while the
target code repositories contain an average of approximately 438k
lines of code, further increasing the difficulty of localization.
‚Ä†Corresponding Author.

ation via LLM-Driven Code
ph Searching

Existing issue localization methods [3, 5, 47] either rely on multi-
stage supervised fine-tuning (SFT) [3] using large-scale training
data or construct complex repository graph indices to support
retrieval-augmented generation through agent-based systems [5,
47]. Still, the following limitations remain: ‚Ě∂ It is difficult to col-
lect sufficient localization data for SFT, and such approaches often
involve substantial training costs. Previous work [3] differentiates
between various stages of hierarchical localization and applies three
rounds of SFT using different techniques. This not only consumes
significant computational resources but also depends on diverse
localization datasets, limiting the generalization. ‚Ě∑ It is difficult to
control the search direction of the agent to ensure precise explo-
ration of the suspicious code snippet. Methods that rely entirely on
LLMs often lack action planning [40], leading to redundant search
behaviors and, in some cases, infinite search loops [48]. On the
other hand, graph index-based searches require maintaining a full
vector representation of the entire code repository [5, 20], which
poses challenges for continuously evolving repositories. ‚Ěł It is
challenging to strike a balance between providing concise, effec-
tive context and covering a sufficiently broad search space. Due
to the limitations of the LLM‚Äôs context window, it is impractical
to supply the entire repository at once. Providing a skeletal struc-
ture [40] helps guide the LLM‚Äôs search and reduce distractions, but
risks omitting key details, especially when tracking affected code
segments that are not directly mentioned [1, 48]. While verbose
contexts may contain richer information, they also introduce a large
amount of irrelevant detail, which can interfere with the LLM‚Äôs
final decision-making.
To overcome the limitations above, we propose CoSIL, an LLM-
driven code repository graph search technique that uses informa-
tion retrieval (IR) to achieve efficient issue localization. CoSIL does
not rely on pre-constructed repository graph indexes. Instead, it
dynamically constructs the graph during the search process by
parsing the target code snippet, with the next step determined by
the LLM, making it a training-free and efficient method. Specifi-
cally, CoSIL employs module call graph enhanced reflection
to identify suspicious code files that are not explicitly mentioned
in the original issue description, thereby constraining the search
space. Furthermore, CoSIL expands the module call graph into a
function call graph and performs an iterative search over it. To
control the search direction and manage the context, we design
a pruner that prevents the LLM from exploring incorrect paths,
thereby enabling effective context pruning.

2.1  Code Graph in Repository
Code repositories often contain rich structural and semantic infor-
mation. To enhance the ability of large language models to infer
implicit relationships between code components within a repos-
itory, researchers have proposed various repository graphs, e.g.,
program dependency graph, to represent the structural information
embedded in code repositories [5, 20, 27, 47]. These code repos-
itory graphs typically describe the invocation and containment
relationships among modules at the class/function level (collec-
tively referred as the function level for simplicity). Based on this,
we will use two types of graphs to represent the code repository
in this work, including the module call graph and the function call
graph.
A code repository consists of multiple code files and resource
files. We only consider code files with actual functional significance
(i.e., modules) in Python repositories. Given a code repository ūĚĎÖ,
the module call graph is defined as GūĚĎÄ (ūĚĎÖ) = (VūĚĎö, EūĚĎö), where
VūĚĎö consists of all modules in ūĚĎÖ, and EūĚĎö includes all "import"
relationships between modules. Furthermore, each node in VūĚĎö can

Jiang et al.
be expanded into multiple class/function nodes. Based on this, the
function call graph can be defined as GūĚźĻ (ūĚĎÖ) = (VūĚĎď , EūĚĎď ), where EūĚĎď
includes all "invoke" and "inherit" relationships between classes
and functions.

2.2  Fault localization
Fault localization aims to identify suspicious functions or state-
ments in the source code that are related to a bug. Existing fault lo-
calization methods include spectrum-based fault localization (SBFL)
techniques [12], mutation-based fault localization (MBFL) tech-

these techniques rely on test cases to trigger fault patterns, and gen-
erating reproduction tests remains a significant challenge [24, 35],
such techniques cannot be effectively applied to GitHub issues.
With the widespread application of large language models (LLMs),
researchers have begun leveraging the powerful reasoning capabil-
ities of LLMs in combination with static analysis tools [13, 31] and
retrieval tools [5, 37, 42], extending fault localization to the repos-
itory level. However, because of the limitations of LLMs in using
external tools, irrelevant information retrieved by incorrect tool
invocations can easily fill the entire context. Inspired by Agentless
[40], in this work, we formulate fault localization as an iterative
search process conducted by an LLM within the call graph G, aim-
ing to identify a subset of the node set V with the top ūĚĎė highest
suspiciousness scores. In this process, each subsequent search step
relies solely on the previous search state, and the call graph will
be constructed and expanded dynamically by LLMs. Moreover, we
only focus on the functions that can be directly invoked in the next
step, ensuring that the search process follows a chain-like pattern
as much as possible.
3  Approach
In this section, we will introduce CoSIL, an LLM-driven issue lo-
calization framework. As shown in Figure 2, CoSIL takes an issue
description and a repository structure tree extracted from the origi-
nal code repository as inputs and finally returns a recommendation
list contained the most suspicious function signatures. It consists
two stages, i.e., file-level search space reduction and function-level

iterative searching. During the file-level stage, CoSIL aids the LLM
by providing a module call graph to help source the suspicious
locations extracted from the issue description (Section 3.1). At the
function-level stage, CoSIL uses a specifically designed search tool
to iteratively explore the function call graph (Section 3.2). Addi-
tionally, CoSIL utilizes a pruning mechanism to regulate both the
direction of graph traversal and the length of the context (Section
3.3). Notably, the call graph is dynamically constructed by the LLM
to avoid redundant context that could otherwise exhaust the context
window.
3.1 Module Call Graph Enhanced Reflection
Because of the limited context window size of LLMs, directly pro-
viding all file-level code to the model is often infeasible. Inspired
by previous studies [27, 40], we recursively traverse the entire
repository and parse it into a hierarchical repository-structure tree
representation. Considering that most issue reports contain can-
didate suspicious location information [48], we first extract the
related files by querying LLM with the issue description. Then, im-
port statements of related files are parsed to construct a first-order
subgraph of the module call graph starting from the corresponding
file nodes, which is then converted into structured textual repre-
sentations. Next, the pre-selected suspicious files, along with the
repository-structure tree and the module call graph, are fed into the
LLM for reflection, enabling the reselection and reranking of the
suspicious files. The rationale behind not utilizing the entire mod-
ule call graph is that the repository-structure tree already provides
a comprehensive view of the repository. Meanwhile, constraining
the search space to as few files as possible facilitates function-level
exploration.

An additional benefit of the reflection step is that, since
parsing the LLM-generated outputs relies on complete and valid file
paths, reflection simultaneously corrects any invalid paths among
the pre-selected files during the re-ranking process.

3.2  Iterative Function Call Graph Searching
Upon obtaining the suspicious files, we first construct a module call
graph among these files and subsequently expand it into a function
call graph, which is seen as the targeted search space, as described
in Section 2.1. Based on the function call graph, we perform an
iterative graph search to collect context and then summarize the
most suspicious classes/functions. Specifically, the function-level
localization consists of the following three steps.
Step I: Initialize the search state. Similar to the repository-
structure tree described in Section 3.1, we construct a file-structure
tree for all suspicious files, outlining all classes, member functions,
and static functions within these files in a hierarchical format. By
providing the issue description along with this file-structure tree
as inputs, the LLM is enabled to preliminarily determine one or
several starting points for the search.
Step II: Iterative search contexts. We set a maximum number
of iterations to prevent the search agent from falling into an infinite
loop. During each iteration step, the search agent first selects a
target node from the set of accessible nodes for exploration. The
target node could be a class node, a class member function node, or
a static function node. Depending on the type of the target node,
the search agent then invokes different search tools shown in Table
1 to retrieve node content, subsequently adding it to the context set.
Once the target node is confirmed and accepted into the context, its
neighbor nodes within the function call graph become accessible
and are added to the candidate set for selection in subsequent
iterations, as shown in algorithm 1. Notably, if the LLM returns an
incorrect function-call parameter, a post-processing procedure will
prompt the LLM to reselect the node for exploration. Additionally,
if the LLM determines that sufficient information has been collected
to support a decision, it will return an "exit" signal, thus terminating
the iteration process prematurely.
Step III: Summarize the suspicious functions. After the
search phase concludes, the search agent incorporates the retrieved.

4.1  Dataset
SWE-bench [11] is the most widely used dataset for evaluating large
language models‚Äô capabilities in real-world software engineering
tasks. It extracts 2,294 tasks from12 Python code repositories, where
each task requires submitting a patch to fix the repositories.
To control experimental costs, we conduct experiments on two
subsets of SWE-bench:
‚ÄĘ SWE-bench Lite [11] is a 300-instance subset selected using
heuristic methods. It removes tasks where the issue description
contains images, external hyperlinks, and other non-textual ele-
ments.
‚ÄĘ SWE-bench Verified [25] is a 500-instance subset developed by
OpenAI. It includes 1,699 manually annotated instances by pro-
fessional annotators and removes instances with underspecified
descriptions or overly specific test cases.
4.2  Research Questions
To evaluate the performance of CoSIL under issue localization and
related tasks, we will consider the following research questions
(RQs).
‚ÄĘ RQ1. Effectiveness: Can CoSIL localize more buggy snippets
in the code repository?
‚ÄĘ RQ2. Guidance: Can the buggy locations found by CoSIL lead
to better performance in issue fixing?
‚ÄĘ RQ3. Ablation: How do the key components contribute to
CoSIL?
4.3  Baselines
We mainly compare CoSIL with two LLM-based open-source local-
ization methods focused on SWE-bench, including Agentless [40]
and Orcaloca [47].
‚ÄĘ Agentless is the current state-of-the-art open-source issue solv-
ing pipeline. It is designed based on human prior knowledge and
achieves the localization process by hierarchically querying the
LLM for suspicious files, functions, and code lines.
‚ÄĘ Orcaloca designs an agent search algorithm based on prior-
ity scheduling, action decomposition, and relevance scoring to
perform searches on the code graph. Additionally, it employs

5.1  RQ1: Effectiveness
To validate the effectiveness of CoSIL in issue localization, we con-
duct experiments based on the Qwen2.5-coder 7B/14B/32B models.
The experimental results on SWE-bench Lite are presented in Table
2, and those on SWE-bench Verified are shown in Table 3.
Experimental results show that on SWE-bench Lite, CoSIL im-
proves file-level localization Top-1 accuracy by 2.9%‚Äď34.6% and
function-level localization top-1 accuracy by 8.6%‚Äď98.2%. On SWE-
bench Verified, the improvements are 5.8%‚Äď11.1% and 10.2%‚Äď85.3%,
respectively. This demonstrates that CoSIL is a localization method
specifically focused on function-granularity search. Additionally,
we examine the probability of different methods generating an
empty localization recommendation list. CoSIL successfully pro-
duces a recommendation list for almost all bugs, indicating the
effectiveness of reflection. Moreover, we observe that compared
to Agentless, OrcaLoca is also a method focused on function-level
localization. This is evident in the fact that even when its file-level
localization results are weaker than those of Agentless, OrcaLoca
still achieves a more accurate function-level recommendation list.
We also observe that when using Qwen2.5-coder-7B, OrcaLoca
achieves the highest file-level top-1 localization success rate.

This
may be because OrcaLoca leverages traceback from reproduction
tests, which provides information independent of the model‚Äôs capa-
bilities.

Moreover, when powered by high-performance models, CoSIL
demonstrates a significant performance gain.

Answer to RQ 1: Compared to other baselines, CoSIL achieves
2.9%-34.6% improvement at file-level and 8.6%-98.2% at function-
level with the lowest empty rate of recommendation list, which
demonstrates its effectiveness.

5.2  RQ2: Guidance
To explore whether more accurate localization information can help
existing software engineering agents improve bug fixing, we use
the function-level localization information obtained in RQ1 with
the Qwen2.5-coder-32B model and apply the Agentless method
for bug fixing. We generate patches using Qwen2.5-coder-32B and
GPT-4o-0806 to examine the relationship between model capabil-
ity differences and the utilization of localization information. The
detailed experimental results are shown in Table 4. The results
indicate that under the same repair conditions, more accurate local-
ization information not only enhances a model‚Äôs ability to generate
successful candidate patches but also improves overall bug-fixing
performance. On SWE-bench Lite, CoSIL increases the resolution
rate by 9.3%‚Äď31.5%, while on SWE-bench Verified, the improvement
is 4.2%‚Äď13.8%.

We observe that CoSIL exhibits greater sensitivity
on SWE-bench Lite, which may be due to the presence of under-
specified issue descriptions in this subset. The localization infor-
mation effectively compensates for these deficiencies, leading to
better performance. Additionally, compared to Qwen2.5-coder-32B,
GPT-4o-0806 demonstrates stronger program repair capabilities,
highlighting the differences in how models inherently understand
and utilize localization information.
In summary, CoSIL enhances the ability of program repair meth-
ods to generate correct patches by providing more accurate lo-
calization information. Moreover, its impact is more pronounced
in higher-performing models, demonstrating not only the effec-
tiveness of issue localization methods but also the critical role of
localization in guiding program repair approaches.

5.3  RQ3: Ablation
To investigate the impact of the three key components in CoSIL on
overall performance, we conduct an ablation study using Qwen2.5-
coder-32B on SWE-bench Lite. For the module call graph, we eval-
uate Top-1, MAP, and MRR at both the file level and function level.
For iterative search and pruning, we only compute function-level
evaluation metrics, as these two components exclusively affect
function-granularity localization.
As shown in Table 5, iterative search is the most critical com-
ponent of CoSIL, and its removal results in approximately a 20%
performance drop. The module call graph primarily functions dur-
ing the reflection phase, and removing it leads to a 10% perfor-
mance loss. Pruning helps prevent context redundancy and guides
the search direction, contributing approximately 3% loss to overall
performance. Specifically:
‚ÄĘ Module Call Graph (Section 3.1): Eliminating the module
call graph essentially removes the file invocation information
provided to the LLM. This causes the LLM to be misled by the
repository structure tree, leading to incorrect identification of
suspicious files. Consequently, the function-level search space
is altered, resulting in a decline in function-level localization
accuracy.
‚ÄĘ Iterive Search (Section 3.2): Eliminating iterative search com-
pletely deprives CoSIL of the ability to obtain context from the
function call graph, reducing it to making direct judgments based
solely on the repository structure tree. Since the repository struc-
ture tree lacks concrete code segment information, it no longer
provides the full context necessary to support decision-making..

6.1  Insights from Generated Patches View
Some studies [21, 40, 47] evaluate localization from the perspective
of the submitted patch, examining the probability that the modifi-
cation location matches the ground truth. They typically use file
match rate and function match rate as evaluation metrics, which
measure the probability that the modified file/function aligns with
the ground truth. To facilitate comparison, we use the GPT-4o-0513
model for localization and generate four sets of candidate patches
using the Agentless method. Each set contains one greedy decoding
sample and nine high-temperature samples. The patches are then
validated and filtered using regression testing and reproduction
tests. We denote this result as CoSIL@40, and the results in Sec-
tion 5.2 are denoted as CoSIL@10, Agentless@10, and Orcaloca@10.
We compare CoSIL@40 with existing GPT-4o-0513 model based
methods in terms of file match rate and function match rate, in-
cluding OpenCSG Starship Agentic Coder [26], Agentless1.5 [40],

AutoCodeRover-v2 [32], Infant Coder [15], RepoGraph [27], Moat-

On one hand, we observe that as localization accuracy improves,
CoSIL@10, Agentless@10, and OrcaLoca@10 exhibit a positive
correlation between file match rate, function match rate, and re-
solved%. On the other hand, we find that Infant-Coder, which has
the highest function match rate, has a resolved% that is 9.7% lower
than OpenCSG, which has the second-highest function match rate.
A similar discrepancy occurs between CoSIL@10, AutoCodeRover,
and RepoGraph, as shown in Table 7. Additionally, we find that
the difference between CoSIL@40 and Agentless1.5 is negligible,
despite CoSIL demonstrating stronger localization capabilities. This
raises an important question: Can function match rate truly reflect
localization performance?
Furthermore, we conduct a detailed comparison between the
official intermediate results provided by Agentless-1.5 and those
generated by CoSIL, including each group of generated samples
and localization results, as shown in Table 8. At the function granu-
larity, CoSIL achieves an absolute localization success rate approx-
imately 15% higher than Agentless-1.5. However, the number of
patch samples generated per group is consistently lower than that
of Agentless-1.5. We hypothesize that this discrepancy is due to
performance variations in the API used.
Undeniably, while issue localization is crucial, effectively utiliz-
ing localization information to generate correct patches remains a
significant challenge. Function match rate-based evaluation is, to
some extent, more susceptible to the influence of the agent-driven
repair stage.
6.2  Threats to Validity
6.2.1  Internal Validity.
‚ÄĘ Hyperparameter settings: We set a maximum iteration count
to control whether the search terminates. Experiments have
verified that CoSIL exhibits different performance levels under
varying iteration counts. However, it remains uncertain whether
the chosen hyperparameters are optimal.

In this section, we will introduce existing works related to CoSIL,
including fault localization algorithms and agentic methods for
issue solving.
7.1  LLM-Based Fault Localization
As large language models (LLMs) continue to improve their code
understanding capabilities, many LLMs have demonstrated remark-
able proficiency in aligning natural language with programming
languages. Wu et al. [39] demonstrate the applicability of LLMs
in fault localization by directly providing erroneous code and logs
to ChatGPT, prompting it to identify defective code lines. LLMAO
[43] trains a lightweight bidirectional adapter within a 128-line
context to generate a suspiciousness score for each line of code,
achieving fine-grained and accurate localization. Qin et al. [31]
propose AgentFL, which integrates LLMs with static analysis tools
to enable repository-level fault localization. Kang et al. [13] lever-
age LLMs‚Äô function-calling capabilities and equipped them with
multiple retrieval tools to enhance their ability to explore code
repositories. Xu et al. [42] and Wang et al. [37] combine static
analysis and code retrieval tools to construct an LLM Agent that
supports decision-making in fault localization.
Recent studies [3, 5, 40, 47] have also focused on the ability
to identify specific code segments based on GitHub issue reports
[11, 45]. Xia et al. [40] propose Agentless, which separates issue
localization from issue fixing and employs hierarchical localiza-
tion by directly prompting LLMs. Based on this, Chang et al. [3]
introduce a method that refines the three levels of localization dif-
ferently based on file structures, function call chains, and program

tless-1.5 on localization and patch generation phases.

dependency graphs, achieving multi-granularity localization per-
formance improvements. Yu et al. [47] propose OrcaLoca, which
utilizes an LLM-based agent to dynamically schedule attention
across different locations and prunes the context based on distance
constraints. Chen et al. [5] propose LocAgent, which integrates
multiple repository graphs and constructs an index, enabling graph
search through various retrieval tools.
Unlike LocAgent [5], CoSIL does not rely on static analysis tools
to precompute a complete repository-wide graph index. Instead,
it dynamically constructs the graph during execution based on
the LLM‚Äôs reasoning while constraining the search space within a
limited range to prevent uncontrolled exploration by the LLM.
7.2  Agentic Methods for Issue Solving
Exploring solutions for software engineering tasks in real-world
code repositories has become a widely studied problem among re-
searchers. SWE-bench [11] is an important benchmark that includes
2,294 programming tasks across 12 repositories. Existing issue solv-
ing methods can be categorized into three main approaches. ‚Ě∂LLM
agent-based methods, such as SWE-Agent [44], AutoCodeRover
[32, 48], Openhands CodeAct [36], and Moatless Tools [2], equip
agents with various tools for accessing and modifying repositories
(e.g., bash, LSP), enabling them to autonomously explore and edit
code files for bug fixing. SWE-Search [1] builds upon this by intro-
ducing Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) to explore the solution
space for optimal fixes, mitigating the uncertainty caused by LLM
sampling. ‚Ě∑ Pipeline-based methods, represented by Agentless
[40], incorporate human prior knowledge by manually identifying
and providing predefined contextual information to assist in fixing
issues. Methods such as RepoGraph [27] and PatchPilot [16] follow
this approach. ‚Ěł Additionally, researchers explore fine-tuning
open-source models for issue solving tasks. SWE-SynInfer [19]
and SWE-Gym [28] demonstrate the effectiveness of fine-tuning
and establish a paradigm for evaluating fine-tuned models on this
task. SWE-Fixer [41] extends this work by introducing a simpler and
more robust method for constructing training data. Furthermore,
SWE-RL [38] and SoRFT [22] explore the potential of reinforcement
learning for fine-tuning in the patch generation stage following
DeepSeek-R1 [6] and make a great improvement.

We propose CoSIL, an LLM-driven, training-free, open-source so-
lution for issue localization. CoSIL performs dynamic graph con-
struction through prompt-based analysis of target code segments. It

constrains the potential search space using a module call graph and
conducts step-by-step iterative search over a function call graph.
A pruning mechanism is introduced to control the search direc-
tion and manage context effectively. On SWE-bench Lite, CoSIL
achieves a 64% success rate in function-level localization. In addi-
tion, we integrate CoSIL with other open-source program repair
methods, resulting in a 4.2%‚Äď31.5% improvement in bug resolution
rate. This demonstrates the guiding role of issue localization in
program repair and contributes to the advancement of automated
software engineering. In the future, we plan to extend CoSIL to sup-
port more programming languages and evaluation benchmarks. We
also aim to enhance software engineering agents‚Äô ability to utilize
localization results more effectively, enabling accurate localization
to better support powerful automated program repair.

CoSIL: Software Issue Localization via LLM-Driven Code Repository Graph Searching
